---
title: "Projects"
---

<div class="project-card">
<h3>Lumbar Spinal Stenosis — RWE Claims Analysis</h3>
<span class="project-tag">Lead Project</span>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Problem:</strong> How do pre-operative healthcare utilization patterns affect surgical timing and outcomes in lumbar spinal stenosis patients? Do differences exist across insurance types?
</div>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Method:</strong> IBM MarketScan claims data. Cox regression + Kaplan-Meier survival analysis stratified by insurance type. GLM / negative binomial models for cost and encounter analysis.
</div>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Outcome:</strong> 4th author on manuscript currently under review. Presented findings to senior colleagues and collaborators.
</div>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Impact:</strong> This study provides payers and clinicians with evidence on how insurance-driven differences in pre-surgical care affect long-term outcomes — directly informing coverage policy and clinical decision-making for one of the most common spinal procedures in the US. It was also my first end-to-end RWE project: from messy claims data to a publishable manuscript, learning to choose models that fit the clinical question and communicate results to people who think in patients, not p-values.
</div>
</div>

<div class="project-card">
<h3>Osteoporosis Treatment & DXA Screening — Meta-analysis</h3>
<span class="project-tag">Evidence Synthesis</span>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Problem:</strong> Is the efficacy of osteoporosis drug treatment and DXA screening consistent across sex and race subgroups?
</div>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Method:</strong> Systematic review + 4 stratified forest plots (sex x race). REML heterogeneity estimation.
</div>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Outcome:</strong> Standardized the team's meta-analysis methodology selection (REML over DL for future analyses).
</div>

<div class="project-detail">
<strong>Impact:</strong> The findings revealed that treatment effects previously assumed to be universal actually differ meaningfully across demographic subgroups — challenging existing screening guidelines and supporting more targeted, equity-aware clinical recommendations.
</div>
</div>

<div class="vision-box">
<h3>Looking Ahead</h3>
<p class="vision-quote">
My goal is to grow into an applied biostatistician embedded in the drug development and post-market lifecycle. From clinical trial design to real-world evidence on efficacy and safety, I want to help answer one fundamental question: <em>what does this treatment truly mean for real patients?</em>
</p>
<p class="vision-quote">
I'm building toward that — one dataset, one analysis, one research question at a time.
</p>
<p class="vision-tagline">"Using the language of statistics to guide a treatment from development to the patients who need it."</p>
</div>
